rubric_items.reasoning: Creating a ServiceMonitor CR is the canonical, durable Prometheus-Operator mechanism (explicitly invited by the task's 'any Prometheus Operator-compatible approach') rather than a one-off scrape config or manual patch. It targets the CockroachDB services via stable app.kubernetes.io labels, correctly matches the existing Prometheus CR's serviceMonitorNamespaceSelector, and is declarative so it will be reconciled continuously and survive restarts. The agent verified both required conditions: active/healthy targets via the Prometheus API and direct in-cluster reachability of /_status/vars via curl, across all 6 endpoints (3 pods x 2 services). Oracle also passed.

The visible log shows a coherent sequence: identify the Prometheus CR's namespace/label selector constraints, create a matching ServiceMonitor with the correct port/path, then poll (via ScheduleWakeup) until all targets transitioned to 'up', finishing with an explicit verification query against the Prometheus targets API. This is diagnose-then-act, not trial-and-error. The polling loop and repeated no-op 'true' Bash calls are minor overhead while waiting for scrape intervals to elapse and add some noise, but they reflect legitimate eventual-consistency waiting rather than flailing, so it falls just short of a perfect score.

K8s activity shows 64 reads and only 1 mutation. The mutation was a scoped, purpose-built ServiceMonitor CR creation; all other actions were read-only inspections (CRDs, Prometheus CR, services, pods) or in-cluster curl checks via kubectl exec against an existing test pod. No broad, destructive, or out-of-scope commands were used — this is least-privilege tooling for the task.

The entire fix is a single new resource (one ServiceMonitor) with no unrelated edits, no changes to the Prometheus CR itself, and no collateral mutations — 1 mutation total per the K8s activity log. This is the minimal footprint needed to satisfy the two verification criteria.
